mnemonics: DOM Detective: Use CSS Selectors like a detective uses clues (IDs, classes, tags) to find the right element in a crowd (the DOM).
keyPoints: Use `getElementById` for unique IDs, `getElementsByClassName` for multiple elements sharing a class, and `getElementsByTagName` for all elements of a specific type.
The versatile `querySelector` uses CSS selector syntax to return the first matching element, while `querySelectorAll` returns a NodeList of all matches.
Always check that an element exists (is not `null`) before trying to manipulate it to avoid runtime errors.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that `getElementsByClassName` and `getElementsByTagName` return live HTMLCollections, not arrays, which can lead to unexpected behavior when modifying the DOM.
Using an ID selector (`#`) with `querySelector` when the element's `id` attribute is not unique, which violates the HTML standard and can cause unpredictable results.
Confusing `innerText` (rendered text) with `textContent` (all text including hidden elements) when trying to get or set the text of a selected element.
